blurb: Mount Teneriffe (4,788 ft) is a forested peak in the western Cascade foothills above North Bend, Washington, rising just northeast of its more famous, smaller neighbor Mount Si within the Washington DNR's Mount Si Natural Resources Conservation Area. Built on Cascade Range bedrock heavily reshaped by Pleistocene glaciation, it is a hiking/scrambling objective (no technical rock, ice, or aid routes are documented) that rewards climbers with broad summit views of Mount Baker, Glacier Peak, and Mount Rainier. The peak takes its name from the island of Tenerife, Spain, and its main trail was rebuilt as a road-to-trail conversion by the Mountains to Sound Greenway Trust to replace an older logging-road approach.

routes.overview: The standard route follows the DNR-built Mount Teneriffe Trail, a long, steadily climbing forest path from the paved trailhead past Teneriffe Falls and over Rachor Pass to a short rocky scramble at the summit — the easiest and most popular way to the top.
routes.beta: A non-technical hike/scramble (Class 1 with a brief Class 2 finish) popular as a longer, quieter alternative to nearby Mount Si. Most of the route follows old logging-road grade and well-graded switchbacks through second-growth forest, with a signed side trip to Teneriffe Falls near the 2.8-mile mark.
routes.approach: From I-90 take Exit 31 into North Bend and continue on North Bend Way; turn left (north) on SE Mount Si Road. Continue about 2.9 miles past the Mount Si trailhead to the paved, ~70-car Mount Teneriffe Trailhead lot (Discover Pass required). From the lot, a half-mile access path switchbacks up through second-growth forest to an old logging-road grade, which climbs gently for about 1.5 miles through young forest and meadow, passing the signed spur down to Teneriffe Falls at roughly 2.8 miles. Beyond the falls junction the trail narrows and climbs via switchbacks (with a Middle Fork Snoqualmie viewpoint) for another ~2.7 miles, then continues about 2.3 more miles, dipping briefly to Rachor Pass (~4,200 ft saddle) before the final short, rocky summit scramble.
routes.descent: Retrace the standard trail back to the trailhead (13 mi round trip); alternatively descend via the Kamikaze Trail for a ~10-mile loop.
routes.descentText: From the summit, downclimb the same short rocky scramble to Rachor Pass, then follow the Mount Teneriffe Trail back down through the switchbacks past the Teneriffe Falls junction to the trailhead, retracing the ascent for roughly 3,800 ft of loss over 6.5 miles. Parties who ascended via the Kamikaze Trail can instead continue down this standard trail through Rachor Pass to close a ~10-mile loop, avoiding the steep, often wet Kamikaze boot track on the way down.
routes.itinerary: Typical day trip: start early morning; ~1.5-2 hrs to Teneriffe Falls (2.8 mi); ~2.5-3 hrs more via the upper switchbacks and Rachor Pass to the summit (6.5 mi, +3,800 ft); 30-45 min on the summit; ~3 hrs back down the same trail. Budget 7-9 hours car-to-car.
routes.bail: Bail is straightforward: the standard trail is a maintained, well-graded path essentially all the way to the final summit scramble, so retreat at any point simply means turning around and descending the same trail.
routes.turnaround: Set a firm turnaround time based on your pace and remaining daylight — the round trip (13 mi / ~3,800 ft) commonly takes 7-9 hours; turn back if you have not reached Rachor Pass by early-to-mid afternoon to avoid descending the falls-area switchbacks after dark.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed roughly June through October, when the upper mountain and final scramble are snow-free. The trail season generally runs mid-March through mid-November depending on snowpack, but expect lingering snow near the ridge crest and the flat below Green Mountain (~4,200 ft) into early summer.
routes.face: North/Northwest approach via Rachor Pass saddle
objHaz: slippery rocks/steep dropoffs near Teneriffe Falls
short exposed rock scramble at the true summit
lingering snow/ice on the upper mountain into early summer
no reliable water source beyond the falls (~2.8 mi in)
routes.detailedRack: No technical protection rack is needed — this is a maintained hiking trail with a short scramble finish, not a roped climb.
routes.proNeeds: None; sturdy footwear and trekking poles are more relevant than any climbing protection.
whatToBring: layers for wind/exposure at the summit
headlamp in case of a late finish
proTips: Arrive early on weekends — the paved ~70-car lot fills by mid-morning in summer.
The final push to the summit is a short but genuine rock scramble; take it slowly if the rock is wet.
Consider descending via the Kamikaze Trail (or vice versa) to make a scenic ~10-mile loop instead of an out-and-back.
watchOut: Slippery rocks and steep dropoffs near Teneriffe Falls, especially hazardous for children.
The rocky summit block requires sure-footed scrambling and careful trampling avoidance of alpine vegetation.
Snow can linger on the upper trail and ridge crest well into late spring/early summer.
knownHazards: Seasonal snow lingers on the upper mountain and ridge crest into June, creating steep, icy sections on an otherwise Class 1-2 trail.
No reliable water sources beyond Teneriffe Falls; carry sufficient water for the full round trip.
The trailhead gate closes seasonally (roughly an hour before dusk); vehicles left past closing risk being locked in.

road.name: SE Mount Si Road
road.seasonalGate: Opens approximately 1 hour after dawn and closes approximately 1 hour before dusk; posted times vary seasonally and are posted at the trailhead.
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 31 (North Bend), follow North Bend Way then turn onto SE Mount Si Road; the paved Mount Teneriffe Trailhead lot is about 2.9 miles past the Mount Si trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWS WAZ303 (Cascades of Southern King County)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate typical of the Cascade west-slope foothills — wet, mild winters and warmer, drier summers.
climate.spring: Lingering snow above roughly 3,500-4,000 ft into May-June; a muddy lower trail is common.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jun-Sep) and the most reliable time for a snow-free summit scramble.
climate.fall: Cooling temperatures with increasing rain by October-November and rapidly shortening days.
climate.winter: Heavy rain at trailhead elevation with snow typically above 2,500-3,000 ft; the lower trail sees winter/snowshoe use, but the summit scramble becomes a snow/ice objective.

access.landManager: Washington State Department of Natural Resources — Mount Si Natural Resources Conservation Area (day-use only)
access.fees: Washington Discover Pass required to park at the trailhead
access.closures: Trailhead access gate closed overnight on a seasonal schedule; trails have been subject to temporary DNR maintenance closures (Mount Si, Little Si, and Mount Teneriffe trails reopened June 18, 2026 after such a closure).

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (6-8am) to beat crowds and afternoon heat

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 206-296-3311 (call 911 for emergencies); King County Search & Rescue is dispatched through KCSO.
emergency.rangerStation: Managed by WA DNR South Puget Sound Region (Natural Areas Program), not USFS; no on-site ranger station. Nearest USFS office for general area info: Snoqualmie Ranger District, 902 SE North Bend Way, North Bend, WA, (425) 888-1421.
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA — (425) 831-2300, 24/7 emergency department.

routes.corrections: Elevation/land-manager check: Wikipedia and Peakbagger both list the managing agency as Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, but per WA DNR's own Mount Si NRCA page, Mount Teneriffe's summit and trail actually lie within the Washington DNR-managed Mount Si Natural Resources Conservation Area, not national forest land. Separately, WTA's trail-stats panel lists a trail 'highest point' of 4,585 ft, but the confirmed summit elevation (matching the existing database value, Wikipedia, and Peakbagger) is 4,788 ft — likely an artifact of WTA's automated elevation-profile tool rather than a real discrepancy in summit height.

routes.overview: A steeper, more direct line that leaves the standard trail near Teneriffe Falls and climbs a boot track up the mountain's southwest (Kamikaze) ridge, gaining roughly 2,300 ft in about a mile before rejoining the upper mountain near the summit — considerably more strenuous than the standard trail.
routes.beta: Popular with scramblers and trail runners seeking a harder version of the peak. Features Class 2-3 rock steps, steep root/dirt tread, and minor exposure, with lingering snow near the ridge crest into early summer. Many parties ascend via Kamikaze and descend the standard trail through Rachor Pass to make a ~10-mile loop rather than retracing the steep boot track.
routes.approach: Follow the standard Mount Teneriffe Trail approach from the paved trailhead on SE Mount Si Road to the signed Teneriffe Falls junction at about 2.8 miles. From near the falls, the unofficial but well-traveled Kamikaze Trail boot track climbs directly up the forested southwest ridge/flank of the mountain, gaining about 2,300 ft in roughly one mile of steep, rocky, single-track tread before reaching the Kamikaze Ridge crest (~3,900 ft) and continuing to a cedar-grove shelf (~4,200 ft) that meets the upper mountain near the summit.
routes.descent: Downclimb the Kamikaze boot track the way you came, or descend via the standard Mount Teneriffe Trail through Rachor Pass for a ~10-mile loop.
routes.descentText: Most parties either carefully retrace the steep Kamikaze boot track (slower and more slip-prone heading down, especially when wet) or continue over the summit onto the standard Mount Teneriffe Trail, dropping to Rachor Pass (~4,200 ft saddle) and following the switchbacks back past the Teneriffe Falls junction to the trailhead for a loop of roughly 10 miles.
routes.itinerary: Typical day trip: start early morning; ~1-1.5 hrs to the Teneriffe Falls/Kamikaze junction (2.8 mi); ~2-2.5 hrs of steep boot-track climbing to the ridge crest and summit (~4.1 mi, +4,000 ft cumulative per trip-report data); short summit break; descend either back down Kamikaze (~2.5 hrs) or via the standard trail loop (~3-3.5 hrs).
routes.bail: The safest bail from partway up the Kamikaze boot track is to downclimb the same way rather than attempting an off-trail traverse over to the standard trail; the steep, loose, sometimes wet tread makes cross-country retreat riskier than retracing known ground.
routes.turnaround: Given the steepness and slip risk of the boot track, set an earlier turnaround than on the standard trail — if you have not reached the ridge crest (~3,900 ft) within the first 2-3 hours, consider retreating the way you came rather than pushing on toward a late, wet descent.
routes.bestSeason: Best climbed roughly June through October; the broader trail-use season runs mid-March through mid-November, but the Kamikaze boot track's steep, wet/mossy rock and lingering snow near the ridge crest make late spring conditions notably harder than on the standard trail.
routes.season: Mar-Nov (best Jun-Oct; snow lingers on the ridge into early summer)
routes.face: Southwest Ridge (Kamikaze Ridge, above Teneriffe/Kamikaze Falls)
objHaz: steep, wet/mossy rock steps on the boot track
minor exposure on the SW ridge
lingering snow/ice near the ridge crest into early summer
no reliable water beyond Teneriffe Falls
routes.detailedRack: No technical protection rack is used — this is an unroped scramble route; microspikes or light traction may be warranted for lingering snow near the ridge crest.
routes.proNeeds: None; sure footing and caution on wet rock substitute for any climbing protection.
whatToBring: layers for wind/exposure on the ridge
headlamp in case of a late finish
proTips: Trip reports describe the Kamikaze Trail as gaining about 2,300 ft in roughly a mile — treat it as a serious workout, not a casual variation.
Ascending Kamikaze and descending the standard trail via Rachor Pass makes a popular ~10-mile loop that avoids downclimbing the steepest, wettest sections.
Microspikes help through the cedar-grove shelf (~4,200 ft) where snow can linger into early summer.
watchOut: Class 2-3 rock steps combined with wet, mossy rock make for a genuinely strenuous, slip-prone scramble.
Minor exposure exists in sections of the boot track — this is not a maintained, graded trail.
Snow lingering near the ridge crest into late spring/early summer can turn easy scrambling into an icy hazard.
knownHazards: The unofficial boot track is steep, root- and rock-covered, and prone to erosion, especially on descent.
Wet or mossy rock on the ridge significantly increases slip risk relative to the standard trail.
Route-finding on the boot track is less obvious than on the signed standard trail, particularly in poor visibility.

road.name: SE Mount Si Road
road.seasonalGate: Opens approximately 1 hour after dawn and closes approximately 1 hour before dusk; posted times vary seasonally and are posted at the trailhead.
road.driveNote: From I-90 Exit 31 (North Bend), follow North Bend Way then turn onto SE Mount Si Road; the paved Mount Teneriffe Trailhead lot is about 2.9 miles past the Mount Si trailhead.

climate.forecastZone: NWS WAZ303 (Cascades of Southern King County)
climate.typical: Marine west-coast climate typical of the Cascade west-slope foothills — wet, mild winters and warmer, drier summers.
climate.spring: Lingering snow above roughly 3,500-4,000 ft into May-June, with steep snow specifically reported on the Kamikaze ridge crest and cedar-grove shelf.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window (Jun-Sep) and the most reliable time for dry rock on the boot track.
climate.fall: Cooling temperatures with increasing rain by October-November; wet rock increases scramble difficulty.
climate.winter: Heavy rain at trailhead elevation with snow typically above 2,500-3,000 ft; the Kamikaze boot track becomes a genuine winter mountaineering line, not a casual scramble.

access.landManager: Washington State Department of Natural Resources — Mount Si Natural Resources Conservation Area (day-use only)
access.fees: Washington Discover Pass required to park at the trailhead
access.closures: Trailhead access gate closed overnight on a seasonal schedule; trails have been subject to temporary DNR maintenance closures (Mount Si, Little Si, and Mount Teneriffe trails reopened June 18, 2026 after such a closure).

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning (6-7am) given the added exertion and slip risk of the boot track

emergency.sheriffDispatch: King County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 206-296-3311 (call 911 for emergencies); King County Search & Rescue is dispatched through KCSO.
emergency.rangerStation: Managed by WA DNR South Puget Sound Region (Natural Areas Program), not USFS; no on-site ranger station. Nearest USFS office for general area info: Snoqualmie Ranger District, 902 SE North Bend Way, North Bend, WA, (425) 888-1421.
emergency.nearestHospital: Snoqualmie Valley Hospital, 9801 Frontier Ave SE, Snoqualmie, WA — (425) 831-2300, 24/7 emergency department.

blurb: Mount Pilchuck (5,324-5,344 ft depending on source) is an isolated summit of quartz monzonite granite rising above the South Fork Stillaguamish River valley near Granite Falls, in the Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest along the Mountain Loop Highway corridor of the western Cascades. It is topped by a historic 1918-1921 U.S. Forest Service fire lookout — now on the National Historic Lookout Register and jointly maintained by Washington State Parks and the Everett Mountaineers — that draws some of the heaviest day-hiker/scrambler traffic of any peak in the state. The mountain was first climbed in 1897 by USGS surveyor Louis Fletcher, decades before any maintained trail existed.
hierarchyNote: Mount Pilchuck's identity is confirmed: its given coordinates (48.057974, -121.797918) match the USGS/Wikipedia summit location exactly, and its elevation is corroborated within normal survey variance (sources cluster 5,324-5,344 ft against the given 5,341 ft — no correction needed). However, the assigned parent "Glacier Peak Region" is geographically questionable: Pilchuck is a standalone peak near Granite Falls/Verlot on the Mountain Loop Highway, roughly 20+ air miles southwest of Glacier Peak itself and well outside the Glacier Peak Wilderness boundary. Guidebooks and land-management sources normally group it with other Mountain Loop Highway / South Fork Stillaguamish peaks (Mount Dickerman, Del Campo Peak, Green Mountain, Bald Mountain) rather than the Glacier Peak massif. This may be an intentional broad regional bucket in the app's hierarchy rather than a data error, but it is worth a hierarchy-team review.

objHaz: Rockfall/loose rock on the summit boulder field
Exposed hands-on scramble and spinning-rung ladder, dangerous when wet or icy
Persistent snow into June/July that can obscure the trail and route markers
Lightning exposure on the open, blasted-rock summit
Heavy crowding on the narrow final scramble and ladder
routes.season: Jul-Oct (snow often lingers on the upper mountain into Jun, sometimes Jul)
routes.overview: The Mount Pilchuck Trail (USFS Trail #700) is the standard, and essentially only maintained, route to the summit: a short, steep old-growth-forest and heather-meadow trail that ends with a boulder-field crossing and a brief hands-on scramble plus short ladder to the historic fire lookout on the rocky summit.
routes.beta: From the FR 42 trailhead the trail climbs through subalpine forest, crosses open talus/boulder slopes marked with orange blazes below the final headwall, and finishes with roughly 20-30 ft of boulder scrambling and a short fixed steel ladder (rungs can spin underfoot) up to the lookout deck. The route is non-technical hiking/scrambling in dry summer conditions but becomes a genuine, consequential snow/ice ascent whenever the upper mountain still holds snow, which in a heavy year can persist into July.
routes.approach: From Granite Falls, follow WA-92 east and then the Mountain Loop Highway roughly 10-11 miles to the Verlot Public Service Center. Continue about 1 mile past Verlot, cross the South Fork Stillaguamish River, and turn right (south) onto Mount Pilchuck Road (FR 42). Follow FR 42 for about 6.8 miles — a mix of paved and rough, washboarded gravel sections that varies by year and maintenance funding — to the Mount Pilchuck Trailhead parking area (roughly 3,000-3,100 ft). In years when FR 42 is gated below the trailhead (common in shoulder season), the hike instead starts from the Heather Lake Trailhead, adding roughly 5 miles and substantial elevation gain each way.
routes.descent: Descend the same Mount Pilchuck Trail back to the FR 42 trailhead; there is no separate descent route.
routes.descentText: From the lookout, reverse the short ladder and boulder-field scramble, then follow the marked trail (orange blazes across the open boulder field, then single-track through forest) back down to the trailhead parking area. Do not attempt to shortcut down the old ski-area clearing visible west of the trail — it looks like a shortcut but is steep and brushy, and has contributed to lost-hiker rescues.
routes.itinerary: Single-day round trip: drive to the FR 42 trailhead, hike up via Trail #700, spend time at the lookout, then descend the same trail — a full day out once drive time from the Seattle/Everett area is included.
routes.bail: The route is a single out-and-back trail with no technical protection; if weather, snow, or fatigue force a retreat, simply turn around and descend the way you came. There is no shorter bail option once on the mountain — the old ski-slope clearing west of the trail is not a safe shortcut and has repeatedly caused SAR callouts for lost hikers.
routes.turnaround: Set a turnaround time before starting and hold to it, especially if snow lingers on the boulder field and final scramble — that terrain becomes materially more dangerous in low light, rain, or refreezing conditions, and the open summit rocks offer no shelter from afternoon thunderstorms.
routes.bestSeason: Best roughly July through early October, once seasonal snow has cleared the upper boulder field and final scramble. The trail is one of the most heavily visited in Washington, so expect company even outside holiday weekends. In many years patchy snow lingers on the upper mountain into late June or July, and the route can start holding new snow again by mid-October, so check recent trip reports before a shoulder-season attempt.
routes.comms: Cell service is unreliable to absent along most of the Mountain Loop Highway approach and on the mountain itself; do not rely on a phone signal in an emergency.
gear: Sturdy hiking boots or approach shoes
Trekking poles for the steep, eroded sections
Microspikes or light traction (commonly needed through June, sometimes into July)
Map/compass or a downloaded GPS track — the boulder-field route-finding is not always obvious
whatToBring: Northwest Forest Pass (or America the Beautiful interagency pass) to park at the trailhead
Extra water — no reliable water sources on the upper mountain
Layers and a rain shell for the exposed, weather-facing summit
Headlamp, given how many parties start late due to trailhead crowding
proTips: Start early on weekends — Pilchuck is one of the most heavily visited hikes in Washington and the trailhead lot fills fast.
The final boulder scramble and ladder rungs can spin underfoot; take your time and let faster parties pass on the narrow sections.
Check recent WTA trip reports before a shoulder-season attempt — snow cover on the boulder field changes the character of the route significantly.
watchOut: Persistent snow/ice on the final boulder scramble and ladder well into early summer has caused serious falls and at least one fatality.
The old ski-area clearing west of the trail looks like a shortcut but is steep and brushy, and has led to lost-hiker rescues.
The open, blasted-rock summit offers no shelter from lightning during afternoon thunderstorms.
knownHazards: Fatal falls have occurred on the exposed final scramble/lookout rocks, including in wet or icy conditions (e.g. a 2015 fall of roughly 400 ft near the lookout).
Snohomish County Volunteer Search and Rescue is called to Mount Pilchuck multiple times per season, mostly for lost or injured hikers on the boulder field and old ski-slope area.
Snow can obscure the trail and boulder-field route markers into early summer, leading to route-finding errors.

pitchDetail.notes: Trailhead through old-growth forest and subalpine meadow to the base of the summit boulder field, roughly 2 miles.
Cross the open talus/boulder field below the summit, following orange trail blazes painted on the rocks.
Roughly 20-30 ft of hands-on boulder scrambling up the summit block, finishing with a short fixed ladder (rungs can spin) onto the lookout deck.

road.name: Mount Pilchuck Road (FR 42)
road.status: Open seasonally, typically summer through mid-fall; pavement/gravel condition mix varies by year and maintenance funding
road.seasonalGate: Gated for winter closure, sometimes at or below the Heather Lake trailhead; reopening date varies with snowpack and Forest Service maintenance
road.driveNote: About 6.8 miles from the Mountain Loop Highway turnoff near Verlot to the trailhead, on a mix of paved and rough gravel/washboard sections; when gated, hikers must start from the Heather Lake Trailhead instead, adding several miles of hiking each way.

climate.typical: Classic wet, maritime western-Cascades weather — frequent low cloud and rain in the shoulder seasons and winter, drier and warmer July through September.
climate.spring: Lingering snowpack on the upper boulder field and final scramble is common into June; the FR 42 access road may still be gated.
climate.summer: Warmest, driest window and by far the most popular time to climb; afternoon thunderstorms are possible on the exposed summit.
climate.fall: Generally good hiking through September into early/mid-October, with increasing chance of new snow and shortening daylight later in the season.
climate.winter: FR 42 is gated; the peak becomes a serious snow ascent with avalanche exposure, outside the scope of the standard hiking route.

access.landManager: Mount Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest (Darrington Ranger District) manages the approach road and lower trail corridor; the upper trail and summit lie within Mount Pilchuck State Park (Washington State Parks), with trail maintenance run jointly with the Everett branch of The Mountaineers.
access.fees: Northwest Forest Pass or America the Beautiful interagency pass required to park at the trailhead.
access.permit: No climbing permit required for the standard route.
access.passRequired: Northwest Forest Pass / America the Beautiful Pass
access.closures: FR 42 is closed seasonally (winter into spring); check Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF alerts before driving out.

timing.recommendedStart: Start by mid-morning at the latest in summer to secure trailhead parking and avoid afternoon thunderstorms on the open summit; start earlier on weekends given how heavily used the trail is.

emergency.sheriffDispatch: Snohomish County Sheriff's Office non-emergency: 425-407-3999; call 911 for emergencies
emergency.rangerStation: Verlot Public Service Center (Darrington Ranger District, Mount Baker-Snoqualmie NF), 33515 Mountain Loop Hwy, Granite Falls, WA, (360) 691-7791
emergency.nearestHospital: Cascade Valley Hospital (Skagit Regional Health), Arlington, WA — nearest ER to the Mountain Loop Highway corridor; Providence Regional Medical Center Everett, Everett, WA, for higher-level trauma care
emergency.notes: Search and rescue on Mount Pilchuck is handled by Snohomish County Volunteer Search & Rescue (SCVSAR); the mountain sees frequent SAR callouts for lost or injured hikers, especially when the boulder field holds snow.

routes.corrections: No data-correcting change was needed for elevation/gain — sources vary naturally (WTA: 5,327 ft summit / 2,300 ft gain / 5.4 mi RT; USFS: 5,324 ft; willhiteweb: 5,327 ft / 2,200 ft gain / ~5 mi RT; Wikipedia: 5,344 ft), and the given 5,341 ft falls within that normal survey-method spread. Mountain Project has no dedicated page for Mount Pilchuck (it is not a technical rock-climbing objective); SummitPost and Peakbagger both blocked automated fetches (HTTP 403) despite repeated attempts, so their content could not be directly verified beyond confirming the pages exist via search results — no facts were sourced from them.

blurb: Chelan Butte is a detached, grassy, sparsely forested summit immediately south of the town of Chelan in north-central Washington's Chelan Mountains group, overlooking Lake Chelan and the Columbia River. It carries no documented technical rock, ice, or alpine climbing route — it was the site of a Washington DNR fire lookout (built 1938, removed from the summit in 1995) and today is one of the Pacific Northwest's most popular hang-gliding and paragliding launch sites, reached by trail or dirt road rather than any climb.
hierarchyNote: Name, coordinates, and elevation all resolve to one real, well-documented peak just south of Chelan, WA, and the wa_sawtooth_chelan parent grouping is geographically plausible for it. Elevation is corrected below (see route corrections). However — this is NOT a technical climbing objective: Mountain Project, SummitPost, and Peakbagger show no rock/ice/alpine climbing route on Chelan Butte. It is a Class 1 walk-up/drivable dirt road (Chelan Butte Trail / Chelan Butte Road), historically a fire-lookout access route and now primarily a hang-gliding/paragliding launch. The single "route" returned below is that non-technical trail, included only because the task asked for the standard route to the summit; every technical-climbing field (rock/ice/alpine grade, pitches, rack, rappels, pitchDetail, aidGrade, commitment) is correctly left null since none of that exists here. Recommend flagging this area for scope review given the app's climbing-only focus — Chelan Butte may not belong in a technical-climbing catalog at all.

objHaz: extreme summer heat and UV exposure on a shadeless slope
dust and loose gravel on the road
seasonal mud and rutting after rain/snowmelt
wildlife-closure period (wintering mule deer) Feb 1 - Mar 31
routes.season: Accessible year-round by road; foot travel discouraged Feb 1 - Mar 31 to protect wintering mule deer
routes.overview: A steep dirt-road/trail walk-up (or drive) up the grassy, exposed south flank of Chelan Butte to a summit crowned by communications towers, with 360-degree views over Lake Chelan, the Columbia River, and the surrounding Chelan/Entiat/Wenatchee Mountains. There is no technical climbing on this route.
routes.beta: The summit can be reached either by hiking the Chelan Butte Trail from a WDFW trailhead on the south side of the town of Chelan (via Iowa Ave, roughly 7.46 mi round trip with 2,500 ft gain per WTA) or by driving/walking Chelan Butte Road from the Millard St trailhead in the Lakeside community (4.5 mi one-way, paved for the first 1.2 mi then dirt/gravel, per Willhite Web). Both converge on the same summit-road system. A bench at the Elephant Head viewpoint offers a rest stop partway up. The route is fully exposed to sun with essentially no shade.
routes.approach: From the town of Chelan, take SR 150 (E Woodin Rd), then Sanders St, Farnham St, S. Saunders St, and E. Iowa Ave to the WDFW Chelan Butte trailhead parking lot, and climb the Chelan Butte Trail directly toward the summit. Alternatively, from Highway 97A at Millard Street in Lakeside (between mileposts 232-233), follow Chelan Butte Road as it climbs the west side of the butte, staying on the road or the marked hiking-trail cutoff about 1/4 mile in.
routes.descent: Reverse the ascent trail or road; the descent is notably easier and faster than the climb.
routes.descentText: Descend the same trail or road used on the way up, passing back through the Elephant Head viewpoint area to the trailhead. No technical downclimbing or rappelling is involved; watch footing on loose gravel and ruts on the road sections, especially after rain or snowmelt.
routes.itinerary: A half-day round trip on foot: most parties cover the roughly 7.5-mile round trip with 2,500 ft of gain in a morning or afternoon outing. The summit can also be reached by vehicle in well under an hour one-way when the road is dry.
routes.bail: Any point on the trail or road is a straightforward bail-out — simply turn around and descend the way you came; there is no technical terrain that requires a specific retreat point.
routes.turnaround: Because extreme heat and UV exposure on this fully shadeless slope are the primary hazard, start early — especially in summer — and turn back if heat, sun exposure, or water supply become limiting before reaching the summit.
routes.bestSeason: Drivable year-round, but best for hiking in spring (wildflowers along the grassy slopes) and fall when temperatures are moderate; summer ascents should start very early to beat the heat and intense UV exposure. Walk-in access is discouraged Feb 1 - Mar 31 to protect wintering mule deer in the Chelan Butte Wildlife Area.
whatToBring: Water — no reliable water source on route
Discover Pass or WDFW Vehicle Access Pass for parking
Leash if bringing a dog (permitted on-leash)
proTips: Hike early morning or evening in summer to avoid the fully shadeless heat
Watch for hang gliders and paragliders launching near the summit on active-flying days
Rest at the Elephant Head viewpoint bench partway up
watchOut: No shade anywhere on the route — heat and UV exposure are the dominant hazard
Road/trail surface can be muddy and rutted after rain or snowmelt
Avoid the area Feb 1 - Mar 31, when walk-in access is discouraged to protect wintering mule deer
knownHazards: Intense sun and UV exposure with significant dust on an entirely shadeless, exposed slope
Loose gravel and a rutted road surface, especially when wet
Active hang-glider and paraglider launch area several hundred feet below and south of the true summit

road.name: Chelan Butte Road
road.status: Paved for the first 1.2 miles from Highway 97A, then dirt and gravel the rest of the way to the summit; can become muddy and rutted seasonally
road.driveNote: 4.5 miles one-way from Highway 97A (Millard St, Lakeside) to the summit; high-clearance recommended when wet

climate.typical: Semi-arid, fully exposed, and hot, with strong summer UV; the road/trail remains accessible year-round
climate.spring: Wildflowers along the grassy slopes; road can still be muddy from snowmelt
climate.summer: Very hot and completely shadeless — start early to avoid peak heat
climate.fall: Cooler temperatures and generally good hiking conditions
climate.winter: Road/trail remain usable for skiing or snowshoeing, but walk-in access is discouraged Feb 1 - Mar 31 to protect wintering mule deer

access.landManager: Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife (Chelan Butte Wildlife Area Unit); the summit access road is county-maintained
access.fees: Discover Pass or a WDFW Vehicle Access Pass is required to park at the WDFW trailhead
access.passRequired: Discover Pass or WDFW Vehicle Access Pass
access.closures: Walk-in access (hiking, snowshoeing, etc.) is discouraged Feb 1 - Mar 31 to protect wintering mule deer

timing.recommendedStart: Early morning, especially in summer, to avoid heat and UV exposure on the fully shadeless slope

emergency.nearestHospital: Lake Chelan Health Hospital, 110 S Apple Blossom Dr, Chelan, WA 98816 — 24-hr ER, Level IV trauma, (509) 682-3300

routes.corrections: Peak elevation corrected: given data listed 3,812 ft, but PeakVisor, Willhite Web, and SummitPost all independently give 3,835 ft (1,169 m) for Chelan Butte's summit — used 3,835 ft as highPointFt. Given coordinates (47.80708, -120.0336815) closely match PeakVisor's sourced summit coordinates (47.806735, -120.034458), so no coordinate correction was made. No technical climbing route exists on this peak in any source checked (Mountain Project, SummitPost, Peakbagger) — see hierarchyNote.
